turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: The claim ┬¡┬¡ everybody concedes that necessary means that there will still be health problems after the Acid Rain Program kicks in.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: All right. And appropriate means?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: The appropriate was intended to meet the claim made by the ┬¡┬¡ the industry that these chemicals already are sufficiently harm┬¡free that we don't need to regulate here regardless of the effects.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Why isn't that part of the first one? I mean ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Well, it could have been done that way. They read them separately ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: ┬¡┬¡ Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: That's the way the government read them.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: It's a silly way to read them, I think.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Perhaps so, Your Honor. But the key thing is, the issues that they were directed to study, the issues that were then supposed to control the listing decision were the health effects of the pollutants that come out of these power plants. And they then deferred the issue of considering a cost to the second stage just as occurs with every single other source of the same 189 hazardous pollutants that they were dealing with. And if I could pause here and just clarify one thing about what happened at that listing stage. Natural gas┬¡fired plants were not turned into a category. They looked at the health effects of natural gas┬¡fired plants and said we are exempting them entirely from regulation under this section, under the appropriate and necessary standard because they simply don't emit these chemicals in more than trace amounts, and there's simply nothing for us to regulate. So natural gas plants get taken out at the listing stage. We then have coal┬¡fired and oil┬¡fired plants as to which they begin to apply the subsection (d) standards, standards which were designed by Congress to limit the emission regulation to reasonable amounts, designed because the floors are, in fact, limited to what has already been achieved by comparable plants in the same category. Now, there was some question raised about whether or not this categorization was something that EPA recognized it could use to effect the emissions standards and make them reasonable. In fact, as Mr. Brownell acknowledged, they did use ┬¡┬¡ create a category ┬¡┬¡ a separate category of coal┬¡burning plants that burned Lignite, because it turns out that none of the Lignite plants could meet the ┬¡┬¡ the standards that would otherwise have ┬¡┬¡ have applied with respect to mercury if they were in the category with the other coal┬¡fired plants. They then through this whole process looked at the issue of categorization. They started out with two coal┬¡fired categories. They ended up with ┬¡┬¡ and as far as that, with one oil┬¡fired category. In the final rule, there are four separate categories of oil┬¡fired plants, depending on what they burn and how they operate. So this whole process of separating out these categories to produce emissions standards that make sense and are practicable was built into that process under subsection (d) ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Smith, could ┬¡┬¡ could you just clarify for me that the ┬¡┬¡ the categorization happens after the listing; is that correct?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. What they listed was all coal┬¡fired plants and all oil┬¡fired plants, but no natural gas plants. They then go to the question of what emissions standards should occur. And with respect to other sources, that's sometimes years after the listing decision is made. But they then have a process of saying, what are our categories going to be? We have to put out ┬¡┬¡ we have to get information at that point. What of the top 12 percent of the category would, in fact, are there emissions? You have to ┬¡┬¡ they have to report in. They make a calculation about that.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Aren't ┬¡┬¡ aren't these just requirements above the minimums that automatically apply?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: No, Your Honor. The ┬¡┬¡ the minimums are established by what the top 12 percent in the category that the EPA has ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Yes. But I'm ┬¡┬¡ I'm saying the categorization that allows you to ┬¡┬¡ you know, to reduce some people and ┬¡┬¡ and not to reduce others, that applies to requirements above the minimums. No?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Your Honor, the ┬¡┬¡ the minimums are the things that are set by mathematical calculation from the categories. Everything else above the minimums is done taking cost into account under (d)(2) expressly.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: So the only thing that is done using not express consideration of cost, but consideration of cost indirectly basing the regulations on what the top 12 percent are doing is the minimums. And the minimums are then altered, depending on what categories you establish. That is the way that the regulation has worked for all of the sources that they regulated. That is the ┬¡┬¡ the practical mechanism ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: So just to clarify that just for ┬¡┬¡ for my own purpose, Mr. Smith. It's just you categorize one way, the minimums are down here. You categorize another way, the minimums are up there.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: It can make a huge difference in terms of what the minimums are.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Right. And there's a notice and comment process. They put out proposed categories. They tell them what we ┬¡┬¡ empirically what we found about what the top 10 ┬¡┬¡ 12 percent are doing. They then get comments in and they ┬¡┬¡ as they happened here ┬¡┬¡ they make different categories in the final rule adjusting.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: How can we ┬¡┬¡ how can we tell the degree to which costs are taken into account, if they are at all, without knowing what the criteria are for creating these subcategories?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, it's in the statute that they create the categories in applying this 12 percent. I don't ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: No, no. The 12 percent ┬¡┬¡ I thought the 12 percent came into play after you've created the category ┬¡┬¡ the subcategory.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Right, as part of the process. It's part of the process.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: But how do I know how they create the subcategories?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Well, it's ┬¡┬¡ you can see it in the notice of proposed rulemaking. They say here are our proposed categories: Two coal categories, one oil category. Then they get comment about how ┬¡┬¡ we are not ┬¡┬¡ what happens, then, is the people that comment say we're so different from that category. We have these special problems. We need our own separate category.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: And it's a rulemaking after the rulemaking that applies to the listing; is that right?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: It's totally after that, Your Honor. It's a second phase. It ┬¡┬¡ it happened here that they announced it all at the same time, but only because there had been an 11┬¡year delay.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: You ┬¡┬¡ you confirm it was not made up in my chambers.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: The fact ┬¡┬¡ the fact is ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Although they did a wonderful job figuring it out again, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: You're right. The ┬¡┬¡ the brief said Congress ┬¡┬¡ the SG's brief ┬¡┬¡ unambiguously required EPA to consider costs at the second stage of the regulatory process. That's what it said. A few pages later, they have the statute. So not surprisingly, I read the statute. And ┬¡┬¡ and reading the statute leads me to think it works along the lines you just said.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: That's correct your honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: But if you did have the most expensive set of generators in the world, you would ask EPA to create a separate category for them in which case the top 12 percent would no longer be in your category and you wouldn't have to do it.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: And that's what happened with ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Now, what I'm asking is if that you think it's the system, that's what I read in the statute. The SG thinks that's the system, but is there a treatise? Is there a ┬¡┬¡ an explanation that EPA has put out so that it is clear that it was not made up, that it's clear that this is the system that they follow? Would you refer me to a source?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Your Honor, the only source that I can refer you to ┬¡┬¡ and perhaps the government can supply something after ┬¡┬¡ is ┬¡┬¡ is the notice of proposed rulemaking in the final rule in which all of this categorization process is laid out in ┬¡┬¡ in exquisite detail. And ┬¡┬¡ and you can see the comments that come in and say the categories don't work, we need different categories, and they then produce different categories.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I think that Justice Alito ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Is there something in the ┬¡┬¡ excuse me. Is there something in the administrative record where EPA adopts that? When somebody says you're not considering cost. It's a bad thing. We're going to go to the Supreme Court if you don't consider cost, and EPA says, oh, no we're going to consider cost. We're going to consider cost when we categorize the power plants. Is there a reference to the administrative record where there's something like that?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Well, they certainly said, Your Honor, in the notice of proposed rulemaking, that we interpret the listing decision as being something that is based solely on health and not on cost.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: The listing decision is not based on cost.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I want to know if there's anything there that says, but don't worry because we're going to consider cost through the categorization process.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: I don't know whether they said that explicitly. That was so implicit in the whole system. This is a system that's been in operation for other sources for ┬¡┬¡ since 1990.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, implicit usually doesn't work when you're talking about an administrative record.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Your Honor, they gave everybody the opportunity to attack the categories that they, in fact, proposed, based on the argument that they were impractical for them.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: But I thought your position was that you didn't need to consider costs at the first step, and that that would include your initial category.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: No, Your Honor, the categories are at the second step. The categories have ┬¡┬¡ the only thing that they did at the first step is say ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: But you didn't take that second step.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: They did, Your Honor. They categorized oil┬¡fired plants into four categories. They categorized coal┬¡fired plants into various categories. And that was all done through a notice and comment process which led, then, to different emission standards.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: And are you saying that was done based on cost?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, it was done ┬¡┬¡ what ┬¡┬¡ what was feasible for these different technologies.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I see. How much money did that save? I mean, do we know how much of the $9.6 billion cost would be reduced by this categorization?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Well, Your Honor ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I mean, that's the problem here, isn't it?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: I don't have that calculus, but I would point out that it's important to recognize that something like 90 percent of that 9.6 billion ┬¡┬¡ 90 percent of the capital cost, which is most of that 9.6 billion, has now already been spent. And the industry has not experienced the kinds of upheavals that are being described. The rule takes effect in the middle of April, and so the idea that the result here was somehow ludicrous or outlandishly expensive is belied by the fact that the industry is bringing itself into full compliance.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Instead of going to jail? Is that it?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Complying with Federal law ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I mean, it might still be ludicrous, but it had to be done.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Well, the other thing I would say is the idea that the $4.6 million benefit is the proper comparator is wrong on so many different levels. First of all, that was a single ┬¡┬¡ one single health benefit related to mercury. They didn't think ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Is the $9 billion a year recurring annually, or are you saying that most of this is capital investment one time?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Most of it is amortization of the capital expenditures that have already been made. That's in all the briefs from the Petitioners. There will be something like 40 percent will be operating costs and 60 percent is amortized capital costs, Your Honor. The industry has been able to do this, and the situation now is we're ready to finally have national standards, which means it's not ┬¡┬¡ the States that have been regulating in this area very strenuously won't continue to have mercury flowing across state lines as they have. And we have this national, highly competitive electricity market, where some companies have marginal costs that reflect that they're in compliance, and some don't. And that is a problem that really needs to be solved.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Smith, I just want you to finish your thought. The four million ┬¡┬¡ the four billion that they are referring to is only mercury. The agency did not quantify all of the other costs for the other HAPs, correct?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Actually, it didn't quantity many, if not most, of the costs for mercury, because mercury causes developmental delays, attention deficit disorder ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, let's put that aside.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: ┬¡┬¡ all these other things that are not quantified in the rule. It causes cardiovascular problems that are not ┬¡┬¡ a lot of things mercury does, it's an extremely poisonous neurotoxin. The other thing about the 30 billion to 90 billion, that is particulate reduction, and some of the particulate that is reduced is, in fact, HAPs. It is nonmercury metals that go out in the form of particles. It is mercury that sometimes goes out in the form of particles. It is the acid gases, which when they get out into the atmosphere turn into particles because they become aerosolized, they go into your lungs as tiny droplets. So all of those are, in fact, being taken care of in the controls of particulate. It is true that in controlling those HAPs, you use the same technology and you end up controlling a lot of other kinds of particulate, primarily sulfur dioxide, which causes premature deaths. And so when they did the calculation, they said we put these particulate controls in to control HAPs. It happens to also save a lot of lives, because of sulfur dioxide that's not otherwise being controlled. And so we'll tell you all the lives we're saving, not just some of those lives.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Was this the basis for the EPA's decision? I thought the EPA ┬¡┬¡ I thought the EPA's position was, it doesn't matter how much the benefit ┬¡┬¡ how much the costs exceed the benefits, we just will not take costs into account at the listing stage.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. They did not consider the cost┬¡benefit analysis at the listing stage. That's based on their reasonable ┬¡┬¡ quite reasonable, I think, interpretation of the statute. The statute, which with respect to every other source, it's conceded you don't do the cost┬¡benefit analysis upfront. This statute came out of a period of regulatory paralysis of 20 years, where the EPA just wasn't regulating effectively. And Congress came in and said we're going to force regulation of these chemicals that are being spewed into the environment and causing all these health effects. It gave one benefit to the power plant industry and said you have three years. You can prove that there wasn't ┬¡┬¡ there aren't really health effects that are serious. But it didn't give them the benefit of having a cost┬¡benefit analysis done upfront, or create all the discretion in the world on the part of EPA simply to say, we don't think you should regulate this particular category.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Smith. Mr. Lindstrom, you have four minutes remaining.

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. Any subcategorization that was going to happen has already occurred, because we're talking about the rule that's been promulgated, and despite any subcategorization that happened, there's still $9.6 billion in costs that are being imposed on an annual basis ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're asking us ┬¡┬¡ it wasn't the question presented. Is the question presented that ┬¡┬¡ not that you have to take that into account at listing, but that somehow that ratio makes any emissions standards wrong ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: The question is whether ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: ┬¡┬¡ even if, for some people, it's really not back┬¡breaking to do it?

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: The question is whether costs have to be considered under (n)(1) when you're regulating electric utilities. And there's one thing I'd like to ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: They do say it does when youÔÇÖre regulating at the emission standard.

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: They say that it's being done ┬¡┬¡ I guess if you think about this, what happens under 7412(c) is you've got a listing decision, first stage. Then you've got a floor standard. And then you've got an above┬¡the┬¡floor standard.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: That's not what ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: In their approach ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're taking out the categorization. They don't establish the floor until they categorize, correct?

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: So categorization could happen here, but my point is, what happens under (n)(1) is these first two steps are merged. You're making the necessary and appropriate determination, when you're looking not to list, but whether such regulation is appropriate and necessary. That's the language in the statute. It says, is such regulation appropriate and necessary. So it's not just whether it's going to be listed. It's looking at what's actually going to happen, so that's why it's ┬¡┬¡ that's why they did both at the same time. Why they made the appropriate and necessary finding at the same time they published the emissions standards. They were looking at the costs ahead because they didn't know what the costs were going to be to figure out if such regulation ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Are you saying that they purported to make the categorization decision without taking into account costs?

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: Yes, any categorization they've done has already been done, and they didn't consider costs.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: And didn't consider cost.

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: That's correct. And again, I'd like to return to one of the big picture principles, which is, this is an administrative law case, so it does ┬¡┬¡ this turns or falls on what the agency actually did below. So they've already actually made determinations. They've said costs are not relevant. They've ignored an important part of the regulatory problem, and adopted ┬¡┬¡

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. They proposed categories, and everybody had the opportunity to say it's the wrong category, correct?

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And argue why it's the wrong category.

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: And the categories have been adopted

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And some people submitted complaints about costs relative to their technology and their kinds of plants, correct?

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So, it's not true that ┬¡┬¡ they proposed them, but everybody gets a chance to tell them, these are ┬¡┬¡ this technology is different from the others, or this kind of plant is different from the others, and it imposes a cost much greater than you are anticipating.

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: I'm saying we're already past that phase. They've already ┬¡┬¡ any categorization they were going to do has already been done.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, what you're saying, it's already past. It's past because that's been ┬¡┬¡ the final rule has been issued.

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm talking during the rulemaking process. The rulemaking process does permit the agency to consider the cost of technology in setting up categorizations.

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: They've adopted the exact opposite position, which is that costs do not matter.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Well, I thought we just heard ┬¡┬¡ I thought we just heard that say the Lignite people, I can't remember if that's the right name, but they said, look, we have special ways of producing this ┬¡┬¡ our stuff, and they are much more expensive, so please don't put us in the same category as you put the other people in. Okay? For the purposes of figuring the best 12 percent, and the agency said, right, okay, separate. Now, did that happen?

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right. Then how would you do that without considering cost? Because their basis was the Lignite people were saying our costs are more expensive?

turns.name: MR. LINDSTROM
turns.text: We don't know how they ┬¡┬¡ I don't know how they did it, but they've said throughout that we're not considering costs. Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
